TextENGNew: In the pitch-black darkness, just after the strange sensation of weightlessness that comes with losing all five senses.
changeReason: The original implies losing senses was caused by the gravity loss; the new translation correctly presents them as co-occurring, matching the Japanese structure.

TextENGNew: I slammed my head against the ground with a dull thud.
A burst of iridescent stars exploded behind my eyelids, and I curled into a ball, groaning as I clutched my aching head.
``A-Are you okay?!@k@vS12/03/180300002. Are you hurt?!``
``Riiikaaa!!@k@vS12/04/180400002. Please answer me!!``
I had no idea why I was in this state, but hearing Mion and the others' voices from the top of the cliff, I understood that I must have tumbled off it.
That's right.
changeReason: The original 'Ah, I remember' implies recollection, but the Japanese そうだ means 'That's right' — an affirmation, not a memory trigger.

TextENGNew: I had been playing with the other club members behind the shrine grounds.
I got carried away and did something reckless... then the branch snapped...
And down I went, headfirst.
changeReason: The new translation adds 'headfirst' which matches 真っ逆さま in the Japanese, a detail absent from the original.

TextENGNew: As if to confirm that, pain began spreading through my entire body after the fact.
changeReason: The new translation captures nuances from the Japanese (それを裏付けるように, 今さら) — the pain serves as confirmation and is notably delayed, both absent in the original.

TextENGNew: But even with my memory returning, something felt strange.
The memories leading up to the moment before I fell from the cliff... no... the 'Rika Furude' from just before the fall -- I couldn't connect with her.
changeReason: The original misinterprets the Japanese: it says she 'couldn't remember who Rika Furude was,' but the Japanese describes being unable to connect with the 'Rika Furude' from before the fall — a discontinuity of identity, not memory loss.

TextENGNew: ``Au... au au au...``
I heard Hanyuu's timid, faltering voice.
changeReason: The original translates おどおど as 'panicked,' but it means 'timid/faltering' — a meaningfully different emotional state.

TextENGNew: Turning around, I saw a figure that matched that voice perfectly -- cowering and uncertain.
``Au au... You were playing with everyone and fell off the cliff...@k@vS12/12/181100003. A-Are you all right...?``
``Hanyuu, ...never mind that.@k@vS12/05/180500005. What day is it?@k@vS12/05/180500006. What year, what month, what day?``
changeReason: The new translation includes 'what day' matching 何日 in the Japanese, which the original omitted, and the opening phrase is closer to the Japanese tone.

TextENGNew: She should have known this is always the very first thing I ask. Yet she never remembers.
changeReason: The original omits Rika's criticism of Hanyuu; the Japanese conveys that Hanyuu should know by now that this is always the first question, yet she never remembers.

TextENGNew: The moment I asked, she seemed to recall that she'd been asked this before.
``Um... let me think... It's June of 1983.@k@vS12/12/181100005. The Watanagashi festival is next week, so... um...``
``...You can't be serious.@k@vS12/05/180500008. ...That's all the time I have...?``
changeReason: The original 'Oh dear' is too mild for 何てこと, which conveys stronger dismay/disbelief; the new 'You can't be serious' better matches Rika's shock at having so little time.

TextENGNew: ``...This is the most we can manage...``
Hanyuu hung her head apologetically.
changeReason: The original says Hanyuu 'muttered regretfully,' but the Japanese describes her hanging her head (俯く) apologetically — a physical action, not speech.

TextENGNew: Mid-June, 1983.
If this truly was the best she could do... then there was nothing to be done about it.@k No point in complaining.
My memories came back, little by little.
``If I'm back in June 1983 again... then I see.@k@vS12/05/180500010. I was killed again.``
After Rena surrendered to the police, everyone in the class should have been examined at the clinic.
changeReason: The original states the checkups as fact; the new translation preserves はずだ ('should have been'), reflecting Rika's inference rather than certainty.

TextENGNew: I hadn't been injured, so I simply marked on the form that I had no wounds, and was allowed to go home.
changeReason: The original implies others determined she was uninjured; the new translation correctly shows Rika herself writing on the questionnaire that she had no injuries, matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: ...From that point on, my memory dissolves into static.@k The kind of memory disruption that accompanies a violent death.
changeReason: The Japanese uses specific terms ノイズ (noise/static) and 記憶混濁 (memory disruption/turbidity); the original's 'vague' loses these technical metaphors that are thematically important to Rika's death-memory concept.

TextENGNew: When life is severed violently and abruptly, the memories immediately preceding death become riddled with noise -- distorted and torn apart.
It's like a roll of toilet paper ripped apart by force... a crass analogy, perhaps, but an apt one.
That's why the memories working backward from the moment of my death that night are drowning in noise, too blurred to make out.
...Only hazy shards of memory remain... something like a handkerchief pressed over my mouth, losing consciousness... I have that vague impression, but nothing certain.
I can only say that it was probably something like that...
In other words, all I know is that I was ambushed on my way home or after I arrived, and killed without warning.
Who killed me and how -- none of that remains in my memory.
...The one small mercy is that the unbearable agony of the moment of death never stays with me either.
changeReason: The original refers to 'the horrific ending to my life,' but the Japanese specifically mentions 耐え難い苦痛 (unbearable agony) of the moment of death — the physical pain, not the ending itself.

TextENGNew: Perhaps because I've experienced this firsthand so many times, I've come to understand why people who die in sudden accidents might wander as ghosts, unaware of their own deaths.
changeReason: The new translation includes 'unaware of their own deaths' (自分の死に気付かず), a key detail the original omitted explaining why ghosts wander.

TextENGNew: If you die at the end of a long hospitalization, the long illness is firmly etched in your memory,@k so you can accept that you must have died in the end.
But when the memory of an ordinary life is torn apart without warning, you can't make sense of anything.
...When I first benefited from this power, I was terribly confused -- wondering if I'd been dreaming,@k or if perhaps I'd hit my head and suffered some kind of amnesia.
changeReason: The original says 'when I first experienced this,' but the Japanese この力の恩恵 specifies 'the benefit of this power,' establishing that the time-loop is a granted ability, not just an experience.

TextENGNew: To calm the confusion, I decided to organize my thoughts.
My name is Rika Furude.
Many people regard me as special, but... that doesn't matter to me.
All I desire is a future where I can live happily with the friends I love.
...How long I've lived in pursuit of even that -- it's hard to remember anymore.
And this girl whose verbal tic is 'au au' is named Hanyuu.
changeReason: The original calls Hanyuu a 'panicked girl,' but the Japanese describes her as the girl whose verbal tic (口癖) is 'au au' — a character trait introduction, not an emotional state.

TextENGNew: She's a being visible only to me.
Or perhaps it would be more accurate to say she's a being that lives inside my head.
In any case, Hanyuu is fundamentally an invisible existence that can only interact with me.
Explaining Hanyuu in full would take far too long, so I'll spare you the details for now.
Besides, even if I tried to explain, I can't think of the right words.
She's been with me since the day I was born -- that's how natural her presence is to me.
Before long, I saw everyone come running up the gravel path.
My beloved, precious friends.
Lively and noisy, throwing themselves wholeheartedly into the most ridiculous endeavors.
Friends I adore, whose company I could never tire of.
``Hau, Rika-chan, are you all right?!@k@vS12/02/180200002. You didn't sprain anything, did you?!``
``Where did you get hurt?@k@vS12/03/180300004. Let this old man take a look!``
``...Mii.@k@vS12/05/180500012. Thank you so much.@k@vS12/05/180500013. I just got a bump on my head.``
changeReason: The original translates Rika's signature vocalization みー as 'Meep,' while the new translation uses 'Mii' which is the standard transliteration used in the fan community.

TextENGNew: Just a bump, I think.@k My injury wasn't nearly as serious as everyone's worried faces suggested.
``When you hit your head, you mustn't let your guard down even if you feel fine at first!``
``Sh-She's right!@k@vS12/01/180100002. Maybe we should have the Coach take a look at you, just to be safe.``
``Rena thinks so too!``
changeReason: The original loses Rena's characteristic third-person self-reference; the Japanese has 'Rena thinks so too' (レナも), which the new translation preserves.

TextENGNew: ``What should we do -- should I go get him?!``
``Yeah.@k@vS12/03/180300006. I'll borrow a phone from a nearby house and call him.``
``...You really don't need to go that far.@k@vS12/05/180500015. See? I'm perfectly fine.@k@vS12/05/180500016. Nipah~``
``Absolutely not, Rika!@k@vS12/04/180400005. You can never be too careful!``
Satoko -- ever the caretaker -- said sternly.
changeReason: The original omits 世話焼きの好きな (who loves being a caretaker), a character detail about Satoko that the new translation preserves.

TextENGNew: ...It really wasn't a serious injury, but since Satoko cared enough to insist, I decided to nod and let her have her way.
changeReason: The original says Satoko was 'trying to be generous,' but the Japanese 顔を立てて means Rika decided to respect/honor Satoko's concern by going along with it.

TextENGNew: Satoko wasn't saying this to act bossy.
She was speaking out of genuine concern for me.
...I'd already seen that across countless worlds.@k So I had no reason to doubt her heart.
changeReason: The original says 'many different lives,' but the Japanese 数多の世界 means 'countless worlds' — a distinction important in Higurashi's narrative where Rika traverses parallel worlds, not just reliving one life.

TextENGNew: But it wasn't only Satoko.
Mion, Rena, and Keiichi were the same.
All of them were truly precious and kind, worrying about me as one of their own.
Mion said she'd go find a phone and dashed off.
Rena took off too, clutching a handkerchief, saying she'd look for a water tap.
Satoko stayed close by, fussing over me anxiously.
Hanyuu just stood there, flustered and helpless.
Keiichi looked worried too, but he was probably telling himself that as the eldest one present, he couldn't afford to lose his composure.
changeReason: The original omits Keiichi's internal reasoning; the Japanese explains he held composure because he was the eldest (年長者) present and felt responsible.

TextENGNew: He stood beside me with a calm expression.
``You okay?``
``...I'm fine.@k@vS12/05/180500018. It doesn't hurt at all anymore.``
``Well, that's a blessing in disguise, at least.@k@vS12/01/180100005. What a relief!``
changeReason: The original adds 'I was super worried!' which isn't in the Japanese; the Japanese 不幸中の幸い (a blessing in disguise) and よかったよかった (what a relief) are about the outcome, not expressing prior worry.

TextENGNew: Had this been the old me, I would have felt nothing special from Keiichi's words.
But... when Rena seized the classroom... when she turned violent against Mion...
Keiichi stood up to it without flinching, trying everything he could to save Rena.
changeReason: The new translation captures 毅然と立ち向かい (confronted resolutely without flinching), a characterization detail about Keiichi's courage that the original's plain 'tried his best' misses.

TextENGNew: He displayed a strength and dependability I could never have imagined from his usual self.
Keiichi is usually tactless -- the kind of person who wounds people's feelings without realizing it. But that's not who he truly is.
He harbors a quiet strength -- the kind of person who would throw himself into harm's way for the sake of his friends.
And I had just 'witnessed' that, hadn't I?
Knowing that was why I could truly feel the warmth behind Keiichi's concern now.
...That's right.
changeReason: Same as earlier: the original translates そうだ as 'Now that I think about it,' but it means 'That's right' — a statement of realization, not contemplation.

TextENGNew: ...In the previous world, Keiichi had revived a 'memory from another world.'
Was it possible... that he still carried that memory in this world too...?
If so...@k That would be the most reassuring thing imaginable.
changeReason: The original's 'I have some hope' dramatically understates この上なく頼もしい ('the most reassuring thing imaginable'), losing Rika's intensity of feeling.

TextENGNew: Hanyuu and I are unbelievably powerless.
After all, Hanyuu is an invisible, intangible existence to everyone but me, and I myself am just a little girl.
There's a limit to what grand maneuvers we can pull off.
But Keiichi is different.
He magnificently shattered the fate that I had surrendered to and given up on.
changeReason: The original's 'changed the fate' loses both 豪快に (magnificently) and 挫け諦めた (that I had broken down and given up on), significantly understating the emotional weight.

TextENGNew: He possesses an unknown power -- one that might smash through the very walls of this labyrinth of fate that holds me captive, and lead me toward a future I've never seen.
changeReason: The original omits the central metaphor: 運命の迷宮を壁ごと打ち破って (smashing through the very walls of the labyrinth of fate), reducing a vivid description of Rika's imprisonment to a generic statement.

TextENGNew: If... Keiichi truly came to understand me...
...there couldn't be a more reassuring ally.
changeReason: The original translates 頼もしい味方 as 'my hero,' but the Japanese means 'a reassuring/dependable ally' — different connotation from a hero figure.

TextENGNew: Then again, perhaps I wasn't thinking that far ahead.
Maybe... I simply wanted someone who could understand the agony of being swallowed by this maze.
changeReason: The original states present desire ('I just want') while the Japanese uses past tense with uncertainty (かも知れない — 'maybe I wanted'), and 'stuck in' loses the violence of 呑み込まれる ('being swallowed by').

TextENGNew: Timidly... I called out to him.
changeReason: The original translates 恐る恐る as 'slowly,' but it means 'timidly/fearfully' — Rika's emotional state when calling out to Keiichi is fear, not deliberateness.

TextENGNew: For a witch who has lived through 1983 for all eternity, to feel this much trepidation about simply calling someone's name... it had been a truly long time.
changeReason: The original omits Rika's self-reference as 魔女 (witch) and the emphasis on the mundane act of 声を掛ける (calling someone's name) being what terrifies her.

TextENGNew: A witch who knows the outcome before she speaks -- she already knows what's inside both the red box and the blue box without opening either.
changeReason: The original omits the red box/blue box metaphor (赤い箱も青い箱も), which illustrates Rika's prescient knowledge as a witch who has seen all outcomes.

TextENGNew: She already knows how trivial the contents are, and has passed beyond disappointment into resigned acceptance.
changeReason: The original misframes the concept: it says Rika 'remains silent' about 'pitiful replies,' but the Japanese describes knowing the trivial contents (下らなさ) and having reached resigned acceptance (諦観) beyond disappointment.

TextENGNew: And yet... for someone like me to be this nervous...
``Hm?@k@vS12/01/180100007. ...Did you call me, Rika-chan?``
Before I could even confirm my faint hope with Keiichi, Hanyuu's face fell into disappointment.
changeReason: The original omits 淡い期待 (faint hope), which reveals Rika's emotional state — she was clinging to a fragile hope that Hanyuu's reaction dashed.

TextENGNew: I didn't need Hanyuu's clouded expression to know.
...That had been an impossible 'miracle.'
If miracles happened so cheaply and easily, they should have occurred frequently in other worlds too.
But I had only ever witnessed that one miracle.
In other words, Keiichi's 'miracle' had a probability of occurring perhaps once -- if at all -- across the mind-numbing span of time I'd been repeating...
changeReason: The original's 'once-in-a-lifetime miracle' underrepresents 気の遠くなるような時間 (a mind-numbingly vast span of time), obscuring the staggering number of loops Rika has endured.

TextENGNew: But it would be a true miracle precisely because something so improbable happened twice in a row...
A world I can't escape just because a die lands on six once.
Unless I achieve a miracle on the order of rolling six on ten dice simultaneously... I can never escape this labyrinth...
changeReason: The original says 'six on the die ten times in a row' (sequential), but the Japanese describes rolling ten dice simultaneously and all showing six — a different probability concept.

TextENGNew: I gripped ten dice in my hand, praying that all of them would land on six... and released them from my palm.
``...Keiichi.``
``What's wrong?@k@vS12/01/180100009. Does something hurt...?``
Because I'd called his name twice, Keiichi looked at me with worry.
``...Keiichi, do... you remember...?``
I knew how abrupt my question was.
...And, exactly as I expected, Keiichi wore a blank, puzzled expression.
``Remember what? ...What are you talking about?``
changeReason: The new translation adds 'Remember what?' (覚えてるって from the Japanese), which the original omitted, and properly formats the dialogue.

TextENGNew: I peered into the depths of Keiichi's eyes -- searching for the true Keiichi that lay within.
changeReason: The original says Rika searched for Keiichi's 'true feelings,' but the Japanese 本当の圭一 means she was looking for 'the true Keiichi' — the version of him who experienced the miracle.

TextENGNew: He should remember... he should know...
Even if he couldn't consciously perceive it, even if the memory wouldn't surface... Keiichi had experienced it...
changeReason: The original collapses two distinct conditions (知覚できなくても — can't perceive, 記憶できなくても — can't remember) into just 'doesn't have the memory,' losing the nuance of unconscious perception.

TextENGNew: ``What's this about, Keiichi-san?@k@vS12/04/180400007. You didn't do something terrible to Rika, did you?@k@vS12/04/180400008. Search your conscience right this instant!``
changeReason: The original translates 自分の胸によく聞いてごらんなさい as 'You had better speak up!' but it means 'Search your own conscience!' — Satoko is telling Keiichi to examine his own guilt, not confess.

TextENGNew: ``...Keiichi... do you remember... climbing onto the school roof...?``
``The school roof?@k@vS12/04/180400010. Keiichi-san, you climbed up there?``
``...Be quiet, Satoko.@k@vS12/05/180500027. Do you remember, Keiichi...?``
``...No, Rika-chan, you must be confusing me with someone else.@k@vS12/01/180100016. I've never climbed on the roof, and I wouldn't even know how.``
...I know perfectly well that the Keiichi of this world has no such experience!
But Keiichi, please, remember...!
Of course you've never actually climbed onto the roof -- but you should remember having done it...!
It's a memory you shouldn't be able to recall... yet you did recall it once before.
So you should be able to recall it again...!
``Maybe you went up there to fetch a ball or something?``
``No, I've never done anything like that.@k@vS12/01/180100018. Have you, Satoko?``
``Certainly not!@k@vS12/04/180400013. The teacher would scold me terribly if I ever did such a thing.``
``Still, I bet it'd feel amazing to take a nap up there!@k@vS12/01/180100020. Ahahahaha!``
``...There's no way... he would remember...``
Guh...
``If miracles happened that easily... neither you nor I... would be suffering like this...``
...Be quiet...
...I knew.
I knew it was probably futile...
``Our fate is set... We're like summer cicadas, doomed to vanish when the festival ends...``
``...Shut up.``
When I turned my fierce glare on her, Hanyuu realized she'd gone too far and shrank back.
It might all be futile.
What imprisons us may not be a labyrinth that at least might have an exit -- but a simple ring with no exit to begin with.
changeReason: The original loses the contrast between a labyrinth (which might have an exit) and a simple ring (輪っか, which has no exit by nature); the new translation preserves this key metaphorical distinction.

TextENGNew: That's right -- we are buried alive by fate...
changeReason: The original says 'trapped in this fate,' but the Japanese 生き埋め means 'buried alive' — a far more violent and desperate image.

TextENGNew: ...That Keiichi... might have been able to pull me free.
But... that Keiichi was... truly a miraculous, fleeting spark.
...The Keiichi of this world was not graced with that miracle.
...But even so... I refuse to give up.
Last time, too... I had given up, resigned to failure.
When I saw that Rena was beyond saving, I said my goodbyes and abandoned that world entirely.
But Keiichi fought -- and shattered the cycle of fate.
With that miraculous Keiichi at my side, couldn't I have fought so much harder?
changeReason: The original states regret ('I should've worked harder'), but the Japanese is a rhetorical question (のではないか) wondering if more was possible, and includes 奇跡の圭一 ('that miraculous Keiichi') which the original omits.

TextENGNew: If I had... couldn't we have shattered so much more of this fate?
changeReason: The original says 'broken this endless cycle' (implying total victory), but the Japanese asks 'couldn't we have shattered so much more of this fate?' — a rhetorical question about incremental progress, not complete escape.

TextENGNew: Am I going to keep repeating this eternal world, hoping for that miracle to happen in Keiichi again...?
No... I can't do that anymore.
I no longer want to keep repeating worlds.
I'm sick of it.
Sick of the world, sick of myself, sick of fate -- sick of everything.@k ...I'm going to drown in destiny...!
changeReason: The original omits 自分にも ('of myself too'); the new translation includes Rika being sick of herself alongside the world and fate, reflecting her self-directed despair.

TextENGNew: Keiichi had wrought a miracle.
Then... I should be able to do the same.
I will fight.@k I will wring out every last drop of my remaining strength and fight.
I've always fought.
I fought even when I had far more power than I do now.
...And I still failed. So what chance do I have now?
But still -- I will fight.
Keiichi taught me that fate can be broken.
So once more -- I will fight.
